title: Generation Inflation
anchor: To understand why younger generations feel financially behind, we need to look at inflation’s outsized role in shaping their reality. Over the past seven decades, the prices of essential goods and services have risen dramatically, far outpacing wage growth.

title: Generation Inflation
anchor: While the cost of living has skyrocketed, wages have failed to keep pace, particularly for middle- and lower-income workers. When adjusted for inflation, real wages reveal a troubling stagnation.

title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
anchor: Introduction

title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
anchor: This essay examines the transformations in American farm labor since 1950, highlighting the forces that shaped its trajectory, the challenges that remain, and the potential impact of LLM-powered humanoid robots.

title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
anchor: Within the next decade, humanoid robots powered by LLMs could take on specialized agricultural tasks, such as picking strawberries, pruning grapevines, or harvesting delicate fruits like cherries. These robots are designed to combine physical dexterity with real-time problem-solving capabilities, enabling them to handle tasks that have historically required skilled human labor.

title: How American Farm Labor is Set to Evolve…
anchor: The introduction of humanoid robots intersects with broader economic and policy challenges. Immigration reform remains critical, as the transition to full automation will take time, leaving an ongoing need for human labor. Programs like the H-2A visa must evolve to address immediate shortages while preparing for future changes.

title: Literacy in The United States and The World
Literacy in The United States and The World
anchor: II. The Current State of Literacy in the United States

title: Literacy in The United States and The World
anchor: The United States faces significant literacy challenges despite decades of educational reforms. According to PIAAC data, twenty-one percent of U.S. adults — representing approximately 43 million people — struggle with low English literacy skills. Within this group, 8.4 million adults score below Level 1, meaning they are functionally illiterate and unable to determine the meaning of sentences or locate single pieces of information in short texts. An additional 26.5 million adults score at Level 1, demonstrating only basic literacy skills, such as identifying vocabulary or deriving meaning from simple sentences. At the higher end, seventy-nine percent of adults score at Level 2 or above, which means they can perform basic comprehension tasks such as paraphrasing and making low-level inferences. These statistics highlight both the progress the U.S. has made and the gaps that persist.

title: Literacy in The United States and The World
anchor: III. The United States in a Global Context

title: Literacy in The United States and The World
anchor: Globally, literacy rates have improved substantially over the last two centuries, with developed nations achieving near-universal basic literacy by the 20th century. A historical overview of global literacy, as illustrated in the Our World in Data chart, shows that nations like the United States and the United Kingdom were early leaders in raising literacy rates, largely due to educational reforms and industrialization. In contrast, regions such as Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia still face substantial challenges, with adult literacy rates in some areas remaining below 60 percent.

Unfortunately, these methods may not fully contain the complexities of self-evolving AI at trillion-parameter scale (for reference, Open AI’s GPT-2 was 1.5 billion, GPT-3 was 175 billion, and GPT-4 was 1.76 trillion).

anchor: Marriage as a Fundamental Right

title: Why Obergefell will not be Overturned
anchor: To overturn Obergefell, the Court would need to revisit not only substantive due process but also long-standing precedents affirming equality under the law — a much broader and more legally fraught endeavor.

title: Why Obergefell will not be Overturned
anchor: Since Obergefell, marriage equality has become a deeply ingrained part of American life. According to a 2023 Gallup poll, 71% of Americans support legal same-sex marriage, a dramatic increase from just two decades ago. Pew Research similarly found that 63% of Americans favor same-sex marriage, with only 34% opposed. This growing acceptance reflects a fundamental shift in societal attitudes, even among conservatives.
